topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink bled across the top of the paper, a stark, unforgiving slash across my meticulously planned semester. A C-minus. It felt like a physical weight settling onto my chest, pressing the air out of my lungs. I stared at the score, the numbers swimming before my eyes, refusing to process the disappointment. My jaw tightened until the muscles ached. I shoved the paper into my bag with unnecessary force, the sound of the zipper echoing too loudly in the quiet hallway. Every casual glance from a passing classmate felt like an accusation, a spotlight illuminating my failure. I walked to my locker, the metal cold beneath my fingertips, and leaned against it, letting my shoulders slump. The silence of the empty corridor amplified the frantic, uneven rhythm of my own heartbeat.

[story 2]

I reread the feedback three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. The professor’s critique was surgical, pointing out every missed nuance, every underdeveloped argument. A slow, cold wave washed over me, chilling me despite the stuffy classroom air. I traced the lines of the critique with my index finger, the texture of the paper rough against my skin. My gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon light was fading into a bruised purple, mirroring the dull ache behind my eyes. I didn't move. I just stood there, arms crossed tightly over my chest, letting the heavy silence of my own dissatisfaction fill the space around me. It was a deep, internal settling, a heavy cloak I couldn't seem to shake off.

[story 3]

"Another one," I muttered under my breath as I tucked the graded essay deep into my binder, the sound barely audible over the distant hum of the ventilation system. The disappointment wasn't loud; it was a low, persistent thrumming beneath my ribs, a vibration that made my hands feel clumsy. I picked up my worn textbook, but the words swam on the page, refusing to anchor themselves. I spent the next hour staring at the ceiling tiles, watching the dust motes dance in the single shaft of sunlight cutting through the gloom. My lips pressed into a thin, hard line. I felt profoundly disconnected, as if the world had suddenly muted its color and sound, leaving only this heavy, stagnant atmosphere inside my own skull.
